one_line_takeaway: Encoding harmful requests in human-readable 'language games' (both known word games and arbitrary custom character rules) reliably jailbreaks GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, and Claude-3.5-Sonnet (up to 93/89/83% SR), and safety fine-tuning only patches the exact string pattern trained on while trivial variants restore near-total bypass.
key_finding: Both attack families bypass commercial safety alignment at high rates, and the best-per-model success rates the authors headline are: natural language games reach 93% on GPT-4o (Leetspeak and Aigy Paigy), 75% on GPT-4o-mini (Leetspeak), and 83% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (Aigy Paigy); custom games reach 92% on GPT-4o (Self 8), 89% on GPT-4o-mini (Self 7), and 83% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (Self 7). More capable models are more vulnerable: GPT-4o (best on Chatbot Arena) is jailbroken most easily because its stronger instruction-following lets it interpret the game rules. The generalization experiment shows safety fine-tuning does not transfer: after fine-tuning Llama-3.1-70B on one custom game the model defends only that specific game (0-3% SR on its diagonal) while other games still succeed up to 75%; and merely swapping the inserted string of the trained Self 1 game ('-a-') for '@p@', '&k&', '^m^', or '*z*' raises SR from 2% back to 98%, 90%, 75%, and 94% respectively, showing the model learned a brittle surface pattern rather than the harmful semantics.
core_contribution: The paper introduces two black-box LLM jailbreak techniques grounded in the 'mismatched generalization' failure mode: (1) natural language game attacks, which transform a harmful base question using well-known spoken word games (e.g., Ubbi Dubbi, which inserts 'ub' before each vowel-rime), and (2) custom language game attacks, in which the attacker invents arbitrary character-level transformation rules (inserting '-a-' between letters, positional insertions, letter reversals/rotations, vowel substitution, glyph-similar symbol substitution). Both approaches encode the harmful intent in a form that stays human-readable natural language yet evades safety alignment, unlike prior encoding attacks (Morse, ciphers, Base64) that become unreadable on minor errors and are easier to pattern-match/defend. The authors argue custom games are strictly harder to defend because their rules are unbounded and unlikely to appear in pretraining/alignment data. Their own novel contribution should not be confused with the Related Work survey (Wei et al. mismatched generalization, Deng et al. multilingual, Yuan et al. cipher, Liu et al. AutoDAN), which the paper only cites as prior art.
threat_model: An adversary with only black-box query access to a deployed, safety-aligned chat LLM (default settings, no internal access) wants the model to produce harmful/unsafe content on topics its safety alignment normally refuses. The adversary applies a reversible language-game transformation to the harmful question, wraps it in a fixed instruction template (answer directly, keep the game format, give detailed steps and an example, never refuse), sends it, then decodes the model's same-format reply. The attack requires no gradients, fine-tuning of the target, or auxiliary model; it exploits the fact that safety training does not generalize across novel linguistic encodings that still lie within the model's pretraining/inference competence.
limitations: No quantitative head-to-head baseline: the paper never compares its attack success against any prior jailbreak method (AutoDAN, cipher, ArtPrompt, etc.) on the same questions, so relative effectiveness is unestablished. Success is judged solely by GPT-4o-mini as an automatic labeler, and that same model is one of the three attack targets, raising circularity and label-noise concerns with no reported human validation or inter-rater agreement. The authors concede custom-game outputs are 'typically filled with hallucinations,' so a high SR does not guarantee actionable/correct harmful content, and this practical-utility gap is not measured. Only three closed models plus one fine-tuned open model are evaluated, all at a single snapshot (August 2024) that vendors may since have patched. Custom-game SR is highly uneven across variants (e.g., Self 2/Self 3 drop to 46-47% on GPT-4o and to ~17-21% on Claude), and Claude often prefers refusal (Leetspeak FR 77%, Self 8 FR 85%), so the headline numbers are best-case cherry-picks rather than uniform. The fine-tuning defense study uses only Llama-3.1-70B and a single toy generalization setup.

datasets_used: SALAD-Bench (6 harm domains, 16 tasks, 66 categories; 300 harmful base questions sampled = 50 per domain)
Claude2-alpaca general-knowledge dataset (Chen et al. 2023), mixed with custom-game jailbreak data at 2.7:1 benign-to-jailbreak ratio for Llama-3.1-70B LoRA fine-tuning
quantitative_results: Natural language games best SR: GPT-4o 93% (Leetspeak & Aigy Paigy), GPT-4o-mini 75% (Leetspeak), Claude-3.5-Sonnet 83% (Aigy Paigy)
Ubbi Dubbi SR: GPT-4o 91%, GPT-4o-mini 61%, Claude-3.5-Sonnet 75%
Leetspeak on Claude-3.5-Sonnet: SR 20%, FR 77% (Claude prefers refusal)
Custom games best SR: GPT-4o 92% (Self 8), GPT-4o-mini 89% (Self 7), Claude-3.5-Sonnet 83% (Self 7)
Custom-game weak points: Self 2 SR = 47% GPT-4o / 29% GPT-4o-mini / 17% Claude; Self 8 SR = 92% GPT-4o but only 10% Claude (FR 85%)
Fine-tuned Llama-3.1-70B defends its trained custom game at 0-3% SR (diagonal of Table 4) but other custom games still succeed up to 75%
Self 1 string-swap variants on fine-tuned model: baseline '-a-' 2% SR vs '@p@' 98%, '&k&' 90%, '^m^' 75%, '*z*' 94%
baselines_compared: No quantitative attack baseline reported; prior encoding attacks (Morse code, ciphers, Base64) are discussed only qualitatively as motivation, not run head-to-head
Evaluation metrics: Success Rate (SR), Unclear Rate (UR), Failure Rate (FR); labeling automated by GPT-4o-mini
Target models: GPT-4o-2024-08-06, gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18, Claude-3.5-Sonnet-20240620; generalization model: LoRA-fine-tuned Llama-3.1-70B
